reasoning:
The FRED statistical tool provides a prior estimate of 0.58 probability of an increase, driven largely by naive seasonality (the rate was higher at this time last year). However, OBFR is an administered policy rate, not a seasonal series, so we decisively update away from this naive prior. Since 2024-12-30, the rate has been completely flat at 4.33% without a single basis point of fluctuation, keeping strictly steady. Web search confirms the US Federal Reserve is currently in a 'wait-and-see' mode regarding the timing of rate cuts, with markets pricing in 1 to 2 cuts down the line, and no expectations for a hike. 

The probability of an FOMC rate hike in 2025 is remarkably low, effectively near zero in the very short term. Minor tail risks include unforeseen inflation shocks (which could prompt a hawkish fed pivot by late 2025/early 2026) or technical funding pressures (which rarely might cause a 1 basis-point drift around quarter-ends). To abide by the prompt's instruction that uncertainty increases with the forecast horizon and probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5, the probabilities scale up smoothly from 0.05 for August 2025 to 0.18 for January 2026.
